summary:
- A: PCR – General term covering many types including reverse-transcription PCR (RT-PCR). While some form of PCR starts with RNA via RT steps, standard PCR amplifies DNA after initial conversion—so not fundamentally 'based' on RNA.
- B: Sanger's technique – Used exclusively for single-stranded nucleic acid sequence determination—it applies solely to double-strand DNA sequences—not applicable to RNA due to lack of stability during processing.
- C: Next-generation sequencing (NGS)—specifically mentioned under “RNA-seq” applications—as part of its scope allows full-length readouts across entire genomes/transcriptomes enabling detailed study of expressed genes/splice sites/variants—all derived directly from raw RNA samples without intermediate conversions.
- D: Western Blotting—is protein-level assay targeting antigens/proteins rather than any genetic material—including no role for either mRNA nor rRNA etc.—thus irrelevant entirely within this context.

Therefore, among these choices, only next-gen sequencing has established protocols involving direct measurement/sampling of native RNAs—for instance analyzing differential expression profiles by quantifying total ribonucleotide content per sample—and thus stands out as being genuinely rooted in working with actual RNA substrates instead relying upon converted intermediaries like cDNA copies made earlier stages.

Conclusion: Based both logically and supported medically/evidence-wise, Option C represents the sole valid candidate meeting criteria defined around active engagement with unconverted primary RNA structures throughout analytical process.

\boxed{C}

- B: Sanger's technique – Used exclusively for single-stranded nucleic acid sequence determination—it applies solely to double-strand DNA sequences—not applicable to RNA due to lack of stability during processing.
- C: Next-generation sequencing (NGS)—specifically mentioned under “RNA-seq” applications—as part of its scope allows full-length readouts across entire genomes/transcriptomes enabling detailed study of expressed genes/splice sites/variants—all derived directly from raw RNA samples without intermediate conversions.
- D: Western Blotting—is protein-level assay targeting antigens/proteins rather than any genetic material—including no role for either mRNA nor rRNA etc.—thus irrelevant entirely within this context.

Therefore, among these choices, only next-gen sequencing has established protocols involving direct measurement/sampling of native RNAs—for instance analyzing differential expression profiles by quantifying total ribonucleotide content per sample—and thus stands out as being genuinely rooted in working with actual RNA substrates instead relying upon converted intermediaries like cDNA copies made earlier stages.

Conclusion: Based both logically and supported medically/evidence-wise, Option C represents the sole valid candidate meeting criteria defined around active engagement with unconverted primary RNA structures throughout analytical process.

\boxed{C}. Answer choice: C
